summary: Monitor detects stale work but primarily alerts/requeues; it does not restart live agents or recover partial failures automatically.
Launcher teardown/restart paths can strand claimed tasks and do not provide a true resume workflow for interrupted runs.
Smoke tests and README do not adequately cover or document actual recovery thresholds and failure-handling behavior.

worker_results.headline: Stuck detection is mtime-based and conservative, but partial failures are not actively recovered.
Teardown and duplicate-session restart can leave claimed tasks stranded; no resume path exists.
Smoke tests do not exercise crashed/stuck worker recovery, restart, or mixed-failure containment.
README recovery docs drift from runtime behavior, especially thresholds and detection mechanism.
Depth/bounding controls exist, but some launch-start failures may not propagate full containment telemetry.

consensus_notes: All five workers found recovery gaps stronger in detection/observability than in automated restart/remediation.
Three workers independently pointed to missing or incomplete handling for interrupted claimed tasks.
Test and documentation coverage both lag the implemented recovery behavior, increasing operational ambiguity.
conflict_notes: No material disagreement on runtime behavior; differences were mainly scope (monitor vs launcher vs docs vs tests).
blockers: No execution blocker. Note: task tool returned inline results without background agent ids, so launch_started telemetry was recorded without agent_id fields.
